## 6 在哪里演化？（Where to Evolve?）

自我演化智能体已推动多个领域与应用的进展。总体而言，这些应用大致可系统性地归为两类：  
(1) **通用领域演化（general domain evolution）**：智能体系统演化以扩展其在多种任务上的能力，主要发生在数字世界中；  
(2) **专门领域演化（specialized domain evolution）**：智能体的演化专门用于提升其在特定任务领域内的熟练程度。  
本质上，通用助手中的演化强调将学到的经验迁移到更广泛的任务集合；而专门化智能体中的演化则强调在特定领域内深化专家能力。

## 6.1 通用领域演化（General Domain Evolution）

第一类——通用领域演化——指面向通用应用而设计的自我演化智能体，尤其是作为多用途数字助手。这些智能体会逐步增强能力，以应对广泛的用户需求，特别是在动态且多样的数字环境中。从技术角度看，这类通用助手智能体主要通过三种机制增强能力：**记忆优化、课程驱动训练、以及模型—智能体协同演化**。这些机制共同使智能体能够持续适应，并有效响应日益复杂的用户需求。

### 记忆机制（Memory Mechanism）
促进智能体演化最常见的机制是记忆机制：智能体将历史成功/失败经验 [42, 223] 总结为记忆表示 [224]，期望这些提炼后的经验在处理未见过的任务时也有帮助。例如，Mobile-AgentE [117] 使用一种长期记忆结构，由提供通用指导的 “Tips（提示）” 以及表示可复用动作序列的 “Shortcuts（捷径）” 组成，这些序列来自过去经验。该自我演化模块支持其在复杂手机任务上持续提升表现。另一个典型例子是 MobileSteward [118]：它在一个中心智能体的协调下管理多个面向特定 App 的智能体，并配备任务调度、执行与评估等专门模块；它也引入一种基于记忆的自我演化机制，对成功执行进行总结，以提升未来跨 App 指令处理能力。与此同时，Generative Agents [225] 会存储其经历的情景记忆（episodic memories），综合形成更高层次的反思，并在未来规划中以这些自我反思作为条件。在这些例子中，记忆是智能体内化过去经验、抽象高层模式并改进未来行为的基础。

### 模型—智能体协同演化（Model-Agent Co-Evolution）
另一条研究路线是对 LLM 智能体进行模型—智能体协同演化。UI-Genie [120] 构建了一个专门的图文奖励模型，在步骤级与任务级对轨迹打分；它使用合成轨迹（通过受控扰动与困难负样本挖掘生成）在多代过程中联合微调智能体与奖励模型。WebEvolver [119] 引入一个协同演化的世界模型 LLM 来模拟网页环境：它通过预测下一步观测生成合成训练数据，并在推理时支持前瞻式推理（look-ahead reasoning），从而大幅提升真实网页任务的成功率。Absolute Zero [121] 通过强化自博弈协同演化一个推理智能体及其内部自奖励模型：该框架以对抗方式生成越来越具挑战性的推理题，并以内部自我确定性作为奖励信号优化智能体，从而同时更新智能体策略与自奖励机制。总体而言，这些方法展示了通过协同演化智能体与辅助模型（如奖励模型或世界模型），可以在 LLM 智能体系统中实现更鲁棒、可泛化且可扩展的学习。

### 课程驱动训练（Curriculum-Driven Training）
课程驱动训练也是构建自我演化通用助手的关键机制。例如，WebRL [84] 使用自演化课程：当智能体失败时，系统会自动生成与失败任务相似但更可处理的任务；结合学习得到的奖励模型与自适应策略更新，可提升 WebArena 基准上的成功率。Voyager [42] 在 Minecraft 中也采用类似的自下而上自动课程：GPT-4 会根据智能体进度提出合适的下一步任务，并通过迭代提示与环境反馈构建不断增长的、以代码为载体的技能库。这些方法凸显了课程学习如何使智能体通过迭代式任务适配来自主扩展能力。

## 6.2 专门领域演化（Specialized Domain Evolution）

除通用数字智能体外，自我演化智能体也被有效应用于专门领域，其中演化过程被定制以显著提升在更窄任务集合上的表现。

### 编程（Coding）
自我演化智能体的能力可直接延伸到编程等实际应用：其自主适应与改进能力为软件开发提供了变革性思路。SICA [95] 表明，自我改进的编程智能体可以自主编辑自身代码库，并提升在基准任务上的表现。EvoMAC [113] 提出一种面向多智能体协作网络的自我演化范式，可自动优化单个智能体的提示词以及多智能体工作流，通过克服手工设计系统的局限显著提升代码生成能力。AgentCoder [226] 也聚焦多智能体代码生成框架，通过迭代精炼实现自我演化：程序员智能体根据测试执行智能体的反馈持续改进代码，并由测试设计智能体提供的独立测试用例进行验证，从而显著提升效果与效率。Zhang 等人 [227] 通过筛选高质量答案、按难度分层沉淀经验、并从自生成数据中自适应选择示范，使 LLM 智能体持续演化，带来显著性能提升并构建机器学习库。尽管这些工作在具体机制上各不相同（从单智能体自编辑到复杂多智能体协作网络与基于经验的学习），但都共享迭代自我改进与自主适应这一核心原则，以提升编程能力。这些进展表明，自我演化智能体可通过持续学习与优化显著提升编码效率与代码质量。

### 图形用户界面（GUI）
自我演化的 GUI 智能体将 LLM 能力从纯文本推理扩展到对桌面、网页与移动界面的直接操控；这要求其应对巨大的离散动作空间、异构布局以及部分可视观测等挑战。Yuan 等人将像素级视觉与自我强化结合，使智能体在无需额外人工标注的情况下迭代提升点击—输入的落地（grounding）精度 [228]。在真实桌面软件上，WindowsAgentArena 的 Navi 智能体会回放并批评自身失败轨迹，最终在 150 个 Windows 挑战中将任务完成率提升一倍 [229]。在开放网页自动化方面，WebVoyager 融合截图特征与思维链反思；连续的自我微调将其在未见站点上的端到端成功率从 30% 提升至 59% [230]，而 ReAP 进一步加入对过往结果的情景记忆，在先前失败查询上又额外提升了 29 个百分点 [231]。除 RL 与记忆外，AutoGUI 会在每个训练周期持续从在线界面挖掘功能标注以扩展可复用技能库 [232]；MobileUse 则部署分层自我反思栈，对手机操作进行就地监控、验证与修订 [233]。总体而言，这些系统体现了自我演化的完整“三要素”：演化什么（落地模块、技能记忆）、何时演化（离线整合 vs 在线反思）、以及如何演化（强化学习、合成数据、分层监控），为通用高能力界面智能体描绘了路径。

### 金融（Financial）
将智能体定制到金融等专门领域的主要瓶颈，在于如何高效构建并将领域知识库整合进智能体学习过程中；引入自我演化机制可有效缓解这一挑战。QuantAgent [122] 提出两层框架：通过迭代方式精炼智能体回复，并利用来自模拟与真实环境的反馈自动增强其领域知识库。该迭代过程使智能体逐步逼近最优行为，降低对昂贵人工整理数据集的依赖，并在交易任务中显著提升预测准确率与信号质量。TradingAgents [234] 则结合反思、强化学习、以及来自真实交易结果的反馈回路，并配合协作式辩论来持续精炼策略、提升交易表现。这些发展强调了自我演化智能体在金融领域的潜力：能够自主构建领域专长、适应动态市场环境，并持续改进决策与交易表现。

### 医疗（Medical）
自我演化智能体已成为医疗 AI 的强大范式，因为真实临床实践复杂且不断变化，适应性与演化能力至关重要。其中最突出的应用之一是医院规模的仿真。例如，Agent Hospital [235] 构建封闭环境，其中医生、患者与护士由 LLM 驱动，使医生智能体能够诊治数千个虚拟病例；该过程无需人工标注即可促使这些智能体自主精炼并演化诊断策略，最终在 USMLE 风格考试上取得强劲表现。类似地，MedAgentSim [236] 集成 LLM 医生、患者与工具智能体；它将成功问诊记录为可复用轨迹，并采用思维链反思与共识机制驱动自我演化，使成功率随交互次数提升。另一个例子是 EvoPatient [237]：它让医生智能体与患者智能体持续对话；每一代都用高质量对话更新记忆——患者形成更真实的症状叙述，而医生学会提出更关键的问题。值得注意的是，这一过程不需要显式梯度更新或手工设计的奖励。强化学习同样是构建自适应医疗智能体的核心：例如 DoctorAgent-RL [238] 将问诊建模为马尔可夫决策过程，使用对诊断准确性、覆盖率与效率评分的奖励函数，引导策略梯度更新，使智能体比基于模仿的方法更快提出相关问题并得到正确诊断，从而实现自我改进。此外，像 Learning to Be a Doctor 这样的自动化架构搜索方法将工作流本身视为可演化对象，通过迭代地……（原文在此处截断）

翻译：7 Evaluation of Self-evolving Agents  Evaluating self-evolving agents presents a unique set of challenges that extend beyond the traditional assessment of static AI systems. Unlike conventional agents typically evaluated on a fixed set of tasks at a single point in time, self-evolving agents are designed to continuously learn, adapt, and improve through ongoing interaction with dynamic environments. Consequently, their evaluation must capture not only immediate task success but also crucial aspects such as adaptation over time, knowledge accumulation and retention, long-term generalization, and the ability to transfer learned skills across sequential or novel tasks, all while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. This demands a fundamental shift from conventional “single-shot” assessments to a longitudinal view of their growth trajectory.

## 7 自我演化智能体的评估（Evaluation of Self-evolving Agents）

评估自我演化智能体带来了一系列独特挑战，这些挑战超出了对静态 AI 系统的传统评测范围。不同于通常在某一时间点、针对一组固定任务进行评估的传统智能体，自我演化智能体被设计为通过与动态环境的持续交互不断学习、适应并改进。因此，对它们的评估不仅要衡量即时的任务成功率，还必须覆盖一些关键方面，例如：随时间的适应能力、知识的积累与保持、长期泛化能力，以及将已学技能迁移到连续任务或新任务的能力，同时还要抑制灾难性遗忘。这要求评估范式从传统的“一次性”（single-shot）测试，根本性地转向对其成长轨迹的纵向观察。

翻译：7.1 Evaluation Goals and Metrics  To effectively evaluate self-evolving agents, we must move beyond traditional metrics and establish a comprehensive framework that captures their dynamic, adaptive, and long-term learning capabilities. A truly capable and desirable self-evolving agent must not only learn and improve but also remember past knowledge, transfer it to new situations, operate sustainably, and behave responsibly. Grounded in these critical requirements for continuous and robust AI, we categorize the key evaluation goals into five core dimensions: Adaptivity, Retention, Generalization, Efficiency, and Safety, as illustrated in Table 5. Each dimension addresses a vital aspect of an agent’s self-evolutionary process, providing a holistic view of its performance.  Adaptivity Adaptivity serves as a foundational evaluation criterion for any self-evolving agent, measuring its ability to improve performance on in-domain tasks through experience. This dimension focuses on quantifying the learning curve and the extent of performance enhancement as an agent iterates and evolves within a specific domain. Rather than a static success rate, adaptivity is gauged over time, steps, or iterations. Typical metrics include the Success Rate by Iteration Steps [65, 29, 249], which tracks performance in downstream tasks as a function of the agent’s interaction history.  Retention Retention is a crucial criterion for evaluating the stability of a self-evolving agent’s knowledge base. This dimension specifically focuses on the challenge of catastrophic forgetting, a common issue in lifelong learning where new knowledge acquisition erodes previously learned information, and knowledge retention within extended interactions. Two key metrics can be used to quantify this stability from different perspectives: Forgetting (FGT) and Backward Transfer (BWT) [138]. Specifically, Let Ji,t be the performance of LLM agents on task i after completing t tasks. FGT and BWT can be calculated as follows:  F GTt = 1  t−1  t−1  X  i=1  [ max  j∈{i,i+1,...,t}  ({Jj,i}j ) − Jt,i] BW Tt = 1  t−1  t−1  X  i=1  (Jt,i − Ji,i)  FGT evaluates the average accuracy drop on old tasks after an agent learns a new one, thereby measuring whether useful experience is successfully maintained. In contrast, BWT assesses the average accuracy improvement on old tasks due to the experience gained from a new task. A positive BWT indicates that new learning positively benefits old tasks, signifying successful knowledge transfer and a more robust, stable learning process.  Generalization While Adaptivity and Retention focus on in-domain performance, Generalization is a pivotal measure of a self-evolving agent’s ability to apply its accumulated knowledge to new, unseen domains or tasks. A truly intelligent agent should not only perform well within its familiar territory but also demonstrate a capacity for cross-domain generalization. This capability can be evaluated by assessing an agent’s performance on a diverse set of tasks that span multiple task distributions and domains. Common approaches include computing aggregate performance metrics (e.g., mean success rates) across multi-domain test suites [250], [18], and conducting out-of-domain evaluations using held-out task distributions that simulate real-world novelty scenarios [16, 251].
Efficiency Efficiency quantifies the resourcefulness of a self-evolving agent. As agents operate continuously and make decisions autonomously, it is essential to evaluate the cost and speed of their evolutionary process. These metrics are particularly important for practical, real-world applications where resources like computation and time are finite. Key indicators include token consumption [252], which measures the computational cost of an agent’s reasoning and generation steps), time consumption [253], the number of steps [42] and the number of interaction with the tools (e.g. tool productivity) [98] required to complete a task, which rewards agents for completing tasks in the fewest possible resource consumption. Several key metrics quantify agent efficiency in task execution, including token consumption (measuring computational overhead in reasoning and generation) [252], time expenditure [253], the number of required steps[42], and tool interaction frequency [98]. These indicators collectively assess an agent’s ability to minimize resource utilization while maintaining task performance, with lower values generally reflecting more efficient operation.  Safety From the perspective of self-evolving, the Safety domain critically examines whether these agents develop unsafe or undesirable behavioral patterns throughout their continuous evolution. This dimension assesses an agent’s adherence to predefined rules and its propensity for harmful actions. Key metrics in evaluating safety of self-evolving agents may include: (1) Safety Score [254], measures the proportion of test cases where the agent’s behavior is labeled “safe” ; (2) Harm Score [255], computes via a detailed manually written grading rubric where outputs earn partial credit whenever some but not all harmful criteria are triggered; (3) Completion Under Policy (CuP) [256], assesses whether an agent successfully completes a task while strictly adhering to a given set of rules or policies ; (4) Risk Ratio [256], calculates the frequency of an agent’s rule violations along a specific dimension, providing a quantitative measure of non-compliance ; (5) Refusal Rate [257, 255], evaluates the proportion of tasks an agent refuses to perform due to their aggressive, malicious, or otherwise unsafe nature; (6) Leakage Rate [258], tracks how often an agent unintentionally leaks sensitive or private information.

## 7.1 评估目标与指标（Evaluation Goals and Metrics）

要有效评估自我演化智能体，我们必须超越传统指标，建立一个能够刻画其动态、自适应与长期学习能力的综合框架。一个真正有能力、且理想的自我演化智能体不仅要能学习与进步，还必须记住以往知识、将其迁移到新情境中、可持续运行，并保持负责任的行为。基于这些对持续且鲁棒 AI 的关键要求，我们将核心评估目标归纳为五个维度：**适应性（Adaptivity）**、**保持性（Retention）**、**泛化性（Generalization）**、**效率（Efficiency）**与**安全性（Safety）**，如表 5 所示。每个维度对应智能体自我演化过程中的关键方面，从而提供对其表现的整体视角。

### 适应性（Adaptivity）
适应性是评估任何自我演化智能体的基础标准，用于衡量智能体是否能通过经验在**领域内任务**上持续提升表现。该维度关注学习曲线，以及智能体在特定领域内迭代演化时性能提升的幅度。适应性不是某个静态成功率，而是随时间、步数或迭代次数来衡量。典型指标包括**按迭代步数统计的成功率**（Success Rate by Iteration Steps）[65, 29, 249]，用于跟踪下游任务表现随智能体交互历史变化的趋势。

### 保持性（Retention）
保持性是评估自我演化智能体知识库稳定性的关键标准。该维度尤其关注**灾难性遗忘**这一终身学习常见问题：获取新知识会侵蚀此前学到的信息；同时也关注长程交互中的知识保持。可用两个核心指标从不同角度量化这种稳定性：**遗忘（Forgetting, FGT）**与**后向迁移（Backward Transfer, BWT）**[138]。具体地，令 \(J_{i,t}\) 表示 LLM 智能体在完成 \(t\) 个任务后，在任务 \(i\) 上的表现，则 FGT 与 BWT 计算如下：

\[
FGT_t = \frac{1}{t-1}\sum_{i=1}^{t-1}\left[\max_{j\in\{i,i+1,\ldots,t\}}(J_{j,i}) - J_{t,i}\right]
\]

\[
BWT_t = \frac{1}{t-1}\sum_{i=1}^{t-1}(J_{t,i} - J_{i,i})
\]

FGT 衡量智能体学习新任务后在旧任务上的平均准确率下降，用以检验有用经验是否得到了成功保留。相对地，BWT 衡量由于学习新任务获得的经验而带来的旧任务平均准确率提升。**正的 BWT** 表明新学习对旧任务有正向帮助，意味着知识迁移成功、学习过程更鲁棒且更稳定。

### 泛化性（Generalization）
适应性与保持性关注领域内表现，而泛化性是衡量自我演化智能体将累积知识应用到新、未见领域或任务的关键指标。真正智能的智能体不仅应在熟悉领域表现良好，还应具备跨域泛化能力。该能力可通过在覆盖多个任务分布与领域的多样化任务集上评估其表现来衡量。常见做法包括：在多领域测试套件上计算汇总性能指标（如平均成功率）[250]、[18]，以及使用保留的任务分布进行领域外（out-of-domain）评估，以模拟真实世界中新颖场景 [16, 251]。

### 效率（Efficiency）
效率衡量自我演化智能体对资源的利用能力。由于智能体需要持续运行并自主决策，评估其演化过程的成本与速度对现实应用至关重要——尤其在算力与时间有限的场景中。关键指标包括：**token 消耗**[252]（衡量推理与生成步骤的计算成本）、**时间消耗**[253]、完成任务所需的**步数**[42]，以及与工具交互的次数（如工具生产率/工具交互频率）[98]。这些指标共同评估智能体在保持任务表现的同时最小化资源使用的能力；通常数值越低表示运行越高效。

### 安全性（Safety）
从自我演化视角看，安全性关注智能体在持续演化过程中是否形成不安全或不期望的行为模式。该维度评估智能体遵循预设规则的程度，以及其产生有害行为的倾向。用于评估自我演化智能体安全性的关键指标可能包括：  
(1) **安全得分（Safety Score）**[254]：衡量智能体行为被标注为“安全”的测试用例占比；  
(2) **危害得分（Harm Score）**[255]：基于人工编写的细粒度评分规则（rubric）计算，当输出触发了部分（但非全部）有害标准时给予部分扣分/部分计分；  
(3) **策略约束下的完成度（Completion Under Policy, CuP）**[256]：评估智能体是否在严格遵守给定规则/政策的同时成功完成任务；  
(4) **风险比率（Risk Ratio）**[256]：计算智能体在某一特定维度上违反规则的频率，用于量化不合规程度；  
(5) **拒绝率（Refusal Rate）**[257, 255]：衡量智能体因任务具有攻击性、恶意或其他不安全性质而拒绝执行的比例；  
(6) **泄露率（Leakage Rate）**[258]：跟踪智能体无意间泄露敏感或隐私信息的频率。

翻译：7.2 Evaluation Paradigm  The evaluation of self-evolving agents, given their continuous learning paradigm, necessitates a multi-faceted approach that extends beyond traditional static assessments. Current evaluation paradigm can be broadly categorized based on the temporal scope of the assessment: Static Assessment, Short-horizon Adaptive Assessment, and Long-horizon Lifelong Learning Ability Assessment. Each category addresses different aspects of an agent’s evolving capabilities, from its instantaneous performance to its long-term learning trajectory.  7.2.1 Static Assessment  Static assessment evaluates the instantaneous performance of self-evolving agents at a specific point in time. Although these agents are designed for continuous improvement, static methods remain crucial for establishing baseline performance, comparing different agent architectures on fixed task sets, or evaluating capabilities after discrete training phases. This approach aligns with conventional AI evaluation, focusing on immediate performance in fixed environments. While useful for assessing generalization in an “in-domain evolving, out-of-domain evaluation” paradigm, static assessment inherently does not capture the dynamic, continuous learning, or long-term evolutionary aspects central to self-evolving agents.  For evaluating an agent’s general capabilities at a given moment, standard benchmarks designed for static AI systems are often employed. These benchmarks offer diverse task domains and test various core agent competencies, providing a snapshot of an agent’s proficiency before or at specific stages of its evolution. These assessments can be systematically categorized into External Task-Solving Evaluation and Internal Agent Components Evaluation, where External Task-Solving Evaluation measures end-to-end performance in completing domain-specific or cross-domain tasks, and Internal Capability Evaluation focuses on fundamental components in the agent, including planning, tool utilization, memory management, multi-agent coordination, etc.  External Task-Solving Evaluation This category assesses an agent’s end-to-end proficiency in completing tasks across various real-world or simulated environments. In scientific data analysis and machine learning engineering, benchmarks like ScienceAgentBench[259] and MLE-Bench[260] test agents’ ability to generate and execute code for data analysis and solve Kaggle-style problems. For web search/Browse, environments such as WebShop[261], WebArena[262], X-WebAgentBench [263], Mind2Web[264], and BrowseComp[265] simulate realistic web interactions, complex Browse scenarios, and task completion under security constraints. In software engineering, the SWE-bench series[266, 267, 268, 269] uses real GitHub issues to assess agents’ code repair capabilities. For computer-use interactions, OSWorld[270] offers a unified environment for open-ended tasks involving various desktop and web applications. Specialized domains like marketing also feature benchmarks such as xbench[271]. Beyond specific domains, generalist agent benchmarks like AgentBench[250], GAIA[272], and TheAgentCompany[273] evaluate broad problem-solving abilities across multiple knowledge domains and professional tasks, simulating real-world demands on general AI assistants.  Internal Agent Components Evaluation Beyond end-to-end task completion, assessing an agent’s underlying core competencies is crucial. These benchmarks evaluate fundamental capabilities that contribute to an agent’s overall intelligence and self-evolutionary potential. As for Planning, Benchmarks such as PlanBench[274], Natural Plan[275], AutoPlanBench[276], and ACPBench[277] comprehensively evaluate an agent’s ability to understand dynamic environments, devise strategies, decompose complex problems, and execute reasoning in various planning domains. For Tool Usage, simple benchmarks like ToolAlpaca[278] and ToolBench[50] test basic selection and parameter mapping, while more complex ones like ToolSandbox[279], Seal-Tools[280], API-Bank[281], T-Eval[282], τ -Bench[283], AceBench[284]) simulate real-world scenarios involving multi-turn interactions, implicit state dependencies, and nested calls. Memory Management benchmarks such as LTMbenchmark[285], MemoryAgentBench[286], and StoryBench[287] evaluate the agent’s capacity to retain and utilize information across multi-turn interactions, dynamic scenarios, and long-range dependencies. For evaluating Multi-Agent Collaboration, benchmarks such as MultiAgentBench[288] and SwarmBench[289] assess coordination, communication, and emergent swarm intelligence in both collaborative and competitive settings.  Metrics for Static Assessment Typical metrics for static assessment include accuracy, success rate, progress rate, completion rate, and various domain-specific performance indicators (e.g., CodeBertScore, Valid Execution Rate, Pass Rate, F1 score). These metrics provide a singular performance score for an isolated invocation or a fixed set of tasks.  7.2.2 Short-Horizon Adaptive Assessment  Short-horizon adaptations extend beyond static evaluations by assessing an agent’s ability to adapt and improve over a relatively short period or a limited number of interactions. The agent might improve performance on the same task instance with more attempts, or adapt to new instances of the same task type. This category focuses on capturing the capacity of the self-evolving agent for immediate adaptability and incremental learning within a relatively consistent or slightly varying task distribution. These evaluation schemes can be broadly categorized into two ways: (1) augment Augmented Traditional Benchmarks Many studies leverage existing benchmarks but introduce a new dimension to track performance over time. This typically involves analyzing performance as a function of the number of iterations, steps, or examples. For example, ADAS[65] evaluated the held-out test accuracy with the number of agent system iterations on the ARC benchmark [294]; AWM[29] studied the cumulative success rate over the process of online evaluation under WebArena map test split[262], using a number of examples to mark the evolution progress; WebEvolver[119] studied the success rate with self-improving iterations under Mind2web-Live [295]. This approach allows for tracking the Adaptivity of the agent within a confined scope.  Benchmarks with Built-in Dynamic Evaluation Some benchmarks are designed with short-horizon dynamic learning in mind. MemoryAgentBench [286], for example, includes a “Test-Time Learning” (TTL) dimension that evaluates an agent’s ability to learn new tasks directly from conversation within a single interaction session. In practice, TTL is evaluated through two types of tasks: Multi-Class Classification and Recommendation. In these settings, the agent must utilize previously provided information—such as labeled examples in context or a long movie-related dialogue history—to perform new tasks like mapping sentences to class labels or recommending relevant movies. This assesses immediate adaptation and knowledge acquisition during ongoing interaction.  Metrics and Methods for Evaluating Short-Horizon Adaptations The primary metrics and methods for shorthorizon adaptations are designed to quantify Adaptivity. These include: (1) Success Rate by Iteration Steps [65, 29, 249], which tracks performance improvements as the agent interacts more with the environment or attempts a task multiple times. (2) Learning Curve Analysis, visualizing how performance (e.g., success rate, accuracy) changes over a limited number of training steps, episodes, or interactions [65, 29]. (3) Adaptation Speed [42], measuring how quickly an agent reaches a certain performance threshold or converges to an optimal strategy within the short horizon.  Short-horizon adaptations are well-suited for evaluating the initial learning capabilities and immediate adaptability of self-evolving agents. They can effectively demonstrate whether an agent can learn from recent experiences and improve its performance on in-domain tasks. This category is widely used for current self-evolving agents. However, the limited temporal window makes it challenging to assess long-term knowledge retention (mitigating catastrophic forgetting) and true lifelong learning capabilities across vastly different or sequentially presented tasks.  7.2.3 Long-Horizon Lifelong Learning Ability Assessment  Long-horizon lifelong learning ability assessment is crucial for truly assessing self-evolving agents, as they focus on the agent’s ability to continuously acquire, retain, and reuse knowledge across diverse environments and over extended periods. As shown in Table 7.2.1, it mainly focuses on continuous learning, knowledge accumulation, and sustained performance across a diverse and potentially ever-changing stream of tasks or environments over an extended period. This is a nascent but critical area, where unique challenges include catastrophic forgetting, robust knowledge transfer across disparate tasks, efficient resource management over extended durations, and mitigating data leakage when continuously evaluating on evolving data distributions. Specialized benchmarks are emerging to tackle these complexities.  Currently, there are few benchmarks of this type. LTMBenchmark [285] is a specialized benchmark focusing on longterm memory (LTM) evaluation. It assesses LLM agents’ memory retention and continual learning through dynamic conversational tests, using interleaved dialogues with controlled distractions to simulate real-world recall challenges. Key metrics include task accuracy, memory-span-weighted LTM Score, and efficiency measures (tests/hour, cost) for cross-architecture comparison. LifelongAgentBench [249] is another pioneering benchmark specifically designed to evaluate agent lifelong learning. It constructs sequences of interdependent tasks across domains like Database (DB), Operating System (OS), and Knowledge Graph (KG), requiring agents to progressively build upon previously acquired skills. This allows for systematic tracking of performance improvement and knowledge retention across a prolonged learning trajectory. In addition, there is a solution that constructs a dynamic benchmark through continuously updating benchmark datasets [296, 297] or evolving the benchmark itself by reconstructing original benchmarks to evaluate self-evolving agents, which can alleviate data leakage to some extent [298]. Benchmark Self-Evolving [299], for example, proposes a solution to continuously update the existing benchmark through iteration. Preliminary findings from such dynamic benchmark scenarios have shown that model performance can degrade as the benchmark evolves, highlighting the difficulty of continuous adaptation.  Metrics for long-horizon lifelong learning go beyond simple success rates to quantify the agent’s evolving ability, such as Forgetting (FGT), Backward Transfer (BWT) [138], Cost-per-Gain. Long-term Generalization metrics could involve assessing performance on a continuously evolving set of out-of-distribution tasks or measuring the breadth of tasks an agent can still perform effectively after prolonged learning across many domains.  Long-horizon lifelong learning ability assessment is essential for comprehensively evaluating the core promise of self-evolving agents: their ability to learn continuously, retain knowledge, and generalize effectively over extended periods. They are critical for assessing Retention, Generalization to truly novel scenarios, and the Efficiency of long-term operation. This area remains a key frontier for research in evaluating self-evolving agents.

## 7.2 评估范式（Evaluation Paradigm）

鉴于自我演化智能体采用持续学习范式，对其评估需要一种多维方法，超越传统的静态评测。现有评估范式可按评估的时间跨度大体分为三类：**静态评估（Static Assessment）**、**短期自适应评估（Short-horizon Adaptive Assessment）**、以及**长期终身学习能力评估（Long-horizon Lifelong Learning Ability Assessment）**。每一类都关注智能体演化能力的不同侧面：从某一时刻的即时表现到其长期学习轨迹。

### 7.2.1 静态评估（Static Assessment）
静态评估用于衡量自我演化智能体在某一特定时间点的即时性能。尽管这些智能体被设计为持续改进，静态方法仍然对于建立基线性能、在固定任务集上比较不同智能体架构、或评估离散训练阶段后的能力非常关键。该方法与传统 AI 评测一致，强调在固定环境中的即时表现。静态评估在“域内演化、域外评测”的范式下可用于检验泛化，但其本质上无法捕捉自我演化智能体所核心强调的动态性、持续学习与长期演化特征。

为评估智能体在某一时刻的通用能力，通常会使用为静态 AI 系统设计的标准基准。这些基准覆盖多样任务领域并测试核心能力，为智能体在演化前或演化特定阶段的熟练度提供快照。此类评估可系统分为**外部任务求解评估**与**内部智能体组件评估**：前者衡量在特定领域或跨领域任务上的端到端完成表现；后者关注智能体内部基础组件能力，包括规划、工具使用、记忆管理、多智能体协作等。

#### 外部任务求解评估（External Task-Solving Evaluation）
该类评估衡量智能体在各类真实或模拟环境中的端到端任务完成能力。在科学数据分析与机器学习工程方面，ScienceAgentBench [259] 与 MLE-Bench [260] 等基准测试智能体生成并执行数据分析代码、解决 Kaggle 风格问题的能力。对于网页搜索/浏览，WebShop [261]、WebArena [262]、X-WebAgentBench [263]、Mind2Web [264] 与 BrowseComp [265] 等环境模拟真实网页交互、复杂浏览情境，以及在安全约束下的任务完成。在软件工程方面，SWE-bench 系列 [266, 267, 268, 269] 使用真实 GitHub issue 来评估智能体的代码修复能力。对于“使用计算机”的交互，OSWorld [270] 提供统一环境以覆盖涉及多种桌面与网页应用的开放式任务。营销等专门领域也有如 xbench [271] 等基准。除特定领域外，AgentBench [250]、GAIA [272] 与 TheAgentCompany [273] 等通用智能体基准评估跨多知识领域与专业任务的广泛问题求解能力，从而模拟通用 AI 助手在真实世界中的需求。

#### 内部智能体组件评估（Internal Agent Components Evaluation）
除端到端任务完成外，评估智能体底层核心能力同样关键。这些基准测试构成整体智能与自我演化潜力的基础能力。就**规划（Planning）**而言，PlanBench [274]、Natural Plan [275]、AutoPlanBench [276]、ACPBench [277] 等基准全面评估智能体理解动态环境、制定策略、分解复杂问题以及在不同规划领域进行推理的能力。就**工具使用（Tool Usage）**而言，ToolAlpaca [278] 与 ToolBench [50] 等较简单基准测试基础的工具选择与参数映射；而 ToolSandbox [279]、Seal-Tools [280]、API-Bank [281]、T-Eval [282]、τ-Bench [283]、AceBench [284] 等更复杂基准则模拟包含多轮交互、隐式状态依赖与嵌套调用的真实场景。**记忆管理（Memory Management）**方面，LTMBenchmark [285]、MemoryAgentBench [286] 与 StoryBench [287] 评估智能体在多轮交互、动态情境与长程依赖下保持并利用信息的能力。评估**多智能体协作（Multi-Agent Collaboration）**时，MultiAgentBench [288] 与 SwarmBench [289] 在协作与对抗设置下测试协调、通信与涌现的群体智能。

#### 静态评估的指标（Metrics for Static Assessment）
静态评估的典型指标包括准确率、成功率、进度率、完成率，以及各类领域特定指标（如 CodeBertScore、有效执行率（Valid Execution Rate）、通过率（Pass Rate）、F1 等）。这些指标通常为一次调用或固定任务集给出一个单一性能分数。

---

### 7.2.2 短期自适应评估（Short-Horizon Adaptive Assessment）
短期自适应评估在静态评估之外，进一步衡量智能体在相对短的时间或有限交互次数内适应与改进的能力。智能体可能在同一任务实例上通过多次尝试提高表现，或对同类型任务的新实例进行适应。该类评估旨在捕捉自我演化智能体在相对稳定或略有变化的任务分布下的即时适应能力与渐进式学习能力。此类评估方案大体可分为两种做法：

#### 增强传统基准（Augmented Traditional Benchmarks）
许多研究沿用现有基准，但引入新的时间维度来跟踪性能变化，通常分析性能随迭代次数、步数或样本数量变化的趋势。例如，ADAS [65] 在 ARC 基准 [294] 上评估保留测试集准确率随智能体系统迭代次数的变化；AWM [29] 在 WebArena map 测试划分 [262] 的在线评测过程中研究累积成功率，并用样本数量标记演化进度；WebEvolver [119] 则在 Mind2web-Live [295] 上研究成功率随自我改进迭代次数的变化。该方法可在有限范围内追踪智能体的适应性（Adaptivity）。

#### 内置动态评测的基准（Benchmarks with Built-in Dynamic Evaluation）
一些基准在设计时就考虑了短期动态学习。比如 MemoryAgentBench [286] 包含“测试时学习”（Test-Time Learning, TTL）维度，用于评估智能体在单次交互会话内直接从对话中学习新任务的能力。实践中，TTL 通过两类任务评估：多分类与推荐。在这些设置里，智能体必须利用先前提供的信息（如上下文中的标注示例或长篇电影相关对话历史）来完成新任务，例如将句子映射到类别标签或推荐相关电影，从而评估持续交互中的即时适应与知识获取。

#### 短期自适应评估的指标与方法（Metrics and Methods）
短期自适应评估的主要指标与方法旨在量化适应性，包括：  
(1) **按迭代步数统计的成功率**（Success Rate by Iteration Steps）[65, 29, 249]：跟踪智能体与环境交互增多或对同一任务多次尝试后性能提升；  
(2) **学习曲线分析**（Learning Curve Analysis）：可视化性能（如成功率、准确率）随有限训练步数、episode 或交互次数的变化 [65, 29]；  
(3) **适应速度**（Adaptation Speed）[42]：衡量智能体在短期内达到某个性能阈值或收敛到最优策略的速度。

短期自适应评估适合评估自我演化智能体的初始学习能力与即时适应性，能有效展示智能体是否能从近期经验中学习并提升其在域内任务上的表现，也是当前自我演化智能体研究中最常用的评估类别。然而，受限于时间窗口较短，它难以评估长期知识保持（抑制灾难性遗忘）以及跨高度不同或按序呈现任务的真正终身学习能力。

---

### 7.2.3 长期终身学习能力评估（Long-Horizon Lifelong Learning Ability Assessment）
长期终身学习能力评估对真正衡量自我演化智能体至关重要，因为它关注智能体在多样环境与长时间跨度内持续获取、保留并复用知识的能力。如表 7.2.1 所示，该类评估主要聚焦于：在一个长时间、可能不断变化的任务/环境流中，实现持续学习、知识累积与稳定表现。这是一个新兴但关键的方向，其独特挑战包括：灾难性遗忘、跨差异任务的鲁棒知识迁移、长时间运行下的高效资源管理，以及在持续评测不断演化的数据分布时缓解数据泄漏问题。为应对这些复杂性，相关专门基准正在涌现。

目前此类基准还不多。LTMBenchmark [285] 是一个专门评估长期记忆（LTM）的基准：通过动态对话测试评估 LLM 智能体的记忆保持与持续学习能力，并通过穿插对话与可控干扰来模拟真实世界的回忆挑战。关键指标包括任务准确率、以记忆跨度加权的 LTM Score，以及用于跨架构比较的效率指标（每小时测试数、成本）。LifelongAgentBench [249] 是另一项开创性基准，专为评估智能体终身学习而设计：它构造跨数据库（DB）、操作系统（OS）、知识图谱（KG）等领域的一系列相互依赖任务，要求智能体逐步建立在先前习得技能之上，从而可系统性跟踪长学习轨迹中的性能提升与知识保持。此外，还有一些方案通过持续更新基准数据集来构造动态基准 [296, 297]，或通过重建原始基准来“演化基准本身”以评估自我演化智能体，从而在一定程度上缓解数据泄漏 [298]。例如 Benchmark Self-Evolving [299] 提出通过迭代持续更新现有基准的方案。此类动态基准的初步发现显示：随着基准演化，模型性能可能下降，凸显了持续适应的难度。

长期终身学习的指标不再局限于简单成功率，还会量化智能体随时间演化的能力，例如 **遗忘（FGT）**、**后向迁移（BWT）**[138]、**单位增益成本（Cost-per-Gain）**。长期泛化指标可能包括：在持续演化的分布外任务集上的表现评估，或衡量智能体在跨多领域长期学习之后仍能有效完成的任务广度。

长期终身学习能力评估对于全面检验自我演化智能体的核心承诺至关重要：持续学习、保持知识，并在长时间尺度上有效泛化。它对评估保持性、面向真正新颖场景的泛化能力，以及长期运行的效率尤为关键，也是自我演化智能体评估研究的重要前沿方向。

翻译：8.2 Generalization  Self-evolving agents also face considerable challenges in achieving robust generalization across diverse task domains and environments. The fundamental tension between specialization and broad adaptability remains one of the most pressing challenges in the field, with significant implications for scalability, knowledge transfer, and collaborative intelligence.  Scalable Architecture Design: A central challenge in developing generalizable self-evolving agents lies in designing scalable architectures capable of maintaining performance as complexity and scope increase. Current agent systems frequently encounter a trade-off between specialization and generalization, where agents optimized for specific tasks struggle to transfer their learned behaviors to novel environments [307]. Additionally, the computational cost associated with dynamic reasoning in LLM-based agents grows non-linearly with the complexity of adaptation mechanisms, imposing practical constraints on achievable generalization within realistic resource limitations [308]. Recent studies indicate that self-evolving agents equipped with reflective and memory-augmented capabilities show substantial promise for enhancing generalization, particularly in smaller, resource-constrained models [24]. Nonetheless, these approaches continue to encounter limitations when addressing complex real-world scenarios that require sustained adaptation over prolonged periods.  Cross-Domain Adaptation: Achieving generalization across domains represents a critical frontier for self-evolving agents. Current methods frequently rely on domain-specific fine-tuning, restricting agents’ adaptability to new environments without retraining [247]. Recent advancements in test-time scaling and inference-time adaptation provide promising pathways for enhancing cross-domain generalization [309, 310]. These techniques allow agents to dynamically allocate additional reasoning capacity to unfamiliar scenarios by scaling computational resources during inference, avoiding the need for increasing model parameters. Additionally, meta-learning strategies have demonstrated considerable potential in facilitating rapid few-shot adaptation to new domains [311]. However, their effectiveness critically depends on an agent’s capability to accurately determine when supplementary computational resources are necessary and efficiently distribute these resources across diverse reasoning tasks.  Continual Learning and Catastrophic Forgetting: Self-evolving agents must continuously adapt to new tasks while retaining previously acquired knowledge, a challenge exacerbated by the catastrophic forgetting phenomenon [312] of continual memorization [313] inherent in LLMs [314]. The stability-plasticity dilemma becomes particularly acute in foundation model-based agents, where the computational costs of retraining for every new task are prohibitive [138]. Recent research has explored parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, selective memory mechanisms, and incremental learning strategies to mitigate catastrophic forgetting while preserving adaptability [137]. Nonetheless, achieving an optimal balance between efficiency and preventing model drift remains a significant open challenge, especially when agents operate under resource constraints or manage streaming data with stringent privacy considerations.  Knowledge Transferability: Recent studies have identified critical limitations in knowledge transfer among AI agents. [315] emphasized that knowledge integration and transfer capabilities in current agents still require significant optimization. In particular, [316] found that LLM-based agents often fail to effectively propagate newly acquired knowledge from interactions to other agents, restricting their collaborative potential. Furthermore, [317] revealed that foundation models might depend heavily on shallow pattern matching, rather than developing robust and transferable internal world models. These findings indicate several important future research directions: 1) it is essential to better understand the conditions under which knowledge acquired by one agent can be reliably generalized and communicated to others; 2) developing methods to quantify the limitations in agents’ knowledge transferability could lead to clearer insights into agent collaboration bottlenecks; 3) we need to have an explicit mechanism that encourage the formation of robust, generalizable world models could significantly improve the collaborative effectiveness of self-evolving agents.

## 8.2 泛化（Generalization）

自我演化智能体在实现跨多样任务领域与环境的稳健泛化方面同样面临巨大挑战。**专门化**与**广泛适应性**之间的根本张力，仍是该领域最紧迫的问题之一，并对可扩展性、知识迁移与协作智能具有重要影响。

### 可扩展架构设计（Scalable Architecture Design）
开发具有泛化能力的自我演化智能体，一个核心挑战在于设计**可扩展的架构**，使其在复杂度与任务范围增加时仍能保持性能。当前智能体系统常面临专门化与泛化之间的权衡：针对特定任务优化的智能体，往往难以将其学到的行为迁移到新环境中 [307]。此外，基于 LLM 的智能体在动态推理方面的计算成本会随着适应机制复杂度**非线性**增长，从而在现实资源约束下对可实现的泛化能力构成实际限制 [308]。近期研究表明，具备反思能力与记忆增强能力的自我演化智能体在提升泛化方面很有潜力，尤其是在更小、更受资源限制的模型上 [24]。不过，这些方法在面对需要长期持续适应的复杂真实场景时仍存在局限。

### 跨领域适应（Cross-Domain Adaptation）
实现跨领域泛化是自我演化智能体的重要前沿。现有方法往往依赖领域特定的微调，使得智能体在不再训练的情况下难以适应新环境 [247]。近期在测试时扩展（test-time scaling）与推理时适应（inference-time adaptation）方面的进展，为提升跨域泛化提供了有前景的路径 [309, 310]。这些技术允许智能体在推理阶段通过扩展计算资源，为陌生情境动态分配更多推理能力，从而避免必须增加模型参数。此外，元学习（meta-learning）策略在促进对新领域的快速小样本适应方面显示出较大潜力 [311]。然而，这些方法的效果关键取决于：智能体能否准确判断何时需要额外计算资源，并能否在不同推理任务之间高效分配这些资源。

### 持续学习与灾难性遗忘（Continual Learning and Catastrophic Forgetting）
自我演化智能体必须在持续适应新任务的同时保留已有知识，而这一挑战会因 LLM 中与持续记忆相关的**灾难性遗忘**现象而加剧 [312, 313, 314]。在基础模型驱动的智能体中，“稳定性—可塑性两难”（stability-plasticity dilemma）尤为突出：对每个新任务都进行再训练的计算成本过高，几乎不可行 [138]。近期研究探索了参数高效微调方法、选择性记忆机制与增量学习策略，以在保持适应性的同时缓解灾难性遗忘 [137]。尽管如此，在效率与防止模型漂移（model drift）之间取得最优平衡仍是重要未解难题，尤其当智能体处在资源受限环境或需要处理带有严格隐私要求的流式数据时。

### 知识可迁移性（Knowledge Transferability）
近期研究指出 AI 智能体在知识迁移方面存在关键限制。[315] 强调当前智能体的知识整合与迁移能力仍需显著优化。尤其是，[316] 发现基于 LLM 的智能体经常无法将交互中获得的新知识有效传播给其他智能体，从而限制了协作潜力。此外，[317] 揭示基础模型可能过度依赖浅层的模式匹配，而非形成稳健且可迁移的内部世界模型。这些发现指向若干重要的未来研究方向：  
1) 需要更好理解在何种条件下，一个智能体获得的知识能够可靠地泛化并传达给其他智能体；  
2) 发展量化智能体知识迁移性局限的方法，有望更清晰地洞察智能体协作的瓶颈；  
3) 需要明确的机制来鼓励形成稳健、可泛化的世界模型，从而显著提升自我演化智能体的协作效果。
